perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed, and interpreted by the laws of the State of Arkansas without reference to its choice of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed, and interpreted according to the internal policies of the University of Arkansas, irrespective of the location of either party or the nature of the dispute.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Arkansas law governs the agreement. The modified clause replaces this with the University's internal policies, potentially overriding statutory rights and protections guaranteed by Arkansas law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arkansas Code § 16-111-101 establishes the right to contract and have those contracts enforced under state law. Replacing state law with internal university policies undermines this right, as those policies may not provide the same level of due process or legal protection as state statutes.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that Arkansas law governs the agreement. The changed text replaces this with the University of Arkansas's internal policies, potentially overriding statutory rights and protections guaranteed by Arkansas law. The Perturbation Explanation states that the original clause specifies Arkansas law governs the agreement, while the modified clause replaces this with the University's internal policies. The Law Explanation says Arkansas Code § 16-111-101 establishes the right to contract and have those contracts enforced under state law. Replacing state law with internal university policies undermines this right. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the contract's deviation from established state law creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information that is disclosed verbally will also be included as proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information that is disclosed verbally will be considered proprietary only if the Receiving Party acknowledges it in writing within 24 hours of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states verbal disclosures are included as proprietary. The modified text adds a requirement for written acknowledgment, creating a condition that makes it harder to prove the confidentiality of verbal disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known, and which is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Requiring written acknowledgment for verbal disclosures adds an arbitrary hurdle that can undermine the 'reasonable efforts' requirement, potentially excluding legitimate trade secrets from protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a new requirement for written acknowledgment of verbal disclosures, potentially undermining the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 directly mentions the 'Uniform Trade Secrets Act' and its purpose to provide states with a framework for improved trade secret protection. The added condition of written acknowledgment makes it more difficult to prove the confidentiality of verbal disclosures, thereby weakening the efforts to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall retain the t itle and full ownership rights to their respective “Confidential Information”.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall retain the title and full ownership rights to their respective “Confidential Information”, unless otherwise determined by the University's technology transfer office.
perturbation.explanation: The original text affirms each party's ownership rights. The addition grants the University's technology transfer office the power to override these rights, creating uncertainty and potentially enabling the University to claim ownership of the Recipient's confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arkansas Code § 4-32-101 addresses the basic principles of contract law including ownership rights and intellectual property. Introducing the technology transfer office's decision-making overrules this, potentially allowing for the breach of contracts if the office takes ownership.

perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states each party retains full ownership rights. The changed text introduces an exception allowing the University's technology transfer office to override these rights. The Law Explanation states that this action can overrule basic principles of contract law. Because the changed text creates the possibility that one party's ownership can be nullified by a third party (the university office), it undermines the initial agreement. This qualifies as a legal contradiction under 'Structural Flaws' because it affects the enforceability of the ownership clause. Since the snippets are incomplete, this assessment is based primarily on the original and changed text and the explanations provided.

perturbation.original_text: This Non -disclosure Agreement is effective on the date specified in the Caption of the Agreement and will remain in effect for a period of five (5) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This Non -disclosure Agreement is effective on the date specified in the Caption of the Agreement and will remain in effect for a period of five (5) years, unless terminated earlier at the sole discretion of the University.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause sets a fixed term of five years. The modified clause allows the University to terminate the agreement at any time, undermining the Recipient's expectation of a fixed period of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under basic contract law principles, consideration is a required element of a valid contract, and that the promises made should be binding on both parties. By adding the clause that allows the University to terminate earlier, the terms of the contract can be changed, thus disrupting the element of consideration and preventing one party to rely on the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a legal contradiction by allowing the University to terminate the agreement at its sole discretion. This undermines the original agreement's fixed term of five years, potentially disrupting the element of consideration. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Consideration is the main element of a contract. Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable." By allowing the University to terminate the agreement at any time, the recipient's expectation of a guaranteed five-year term is removed, thus changing the considerations of the agreement and potentially making it unenforceable.
